label: CSUS Crisis Assistance Resource Education Support

pages.title: Crisis Assistance & Resource Education Support (CARES) | Sacramento State
pages.description: The CARES office provides support to students who are having a difficult time with their basic needs like finances, food, and counseling.
headings: Crisis Assistance & Resource Education Support (CARES) Division of Student Affairs
About Crisis Assistance & Resource Education Support (CARES)
Help is just a visit, a phone call or an email away
Connect with the Sac State Crisis Assistance & Resource Education office
contentBlocks: The Sacramento State CARES office provides support to students who are in crisis or experiencing unique challenges to their education. We coordinate referrals to campus and community resources and offer follow-up support to address a variety of issues including, but not limited to:
Housing, Food & Transportation Resources
Academic Case Management & Faculty Liaison Support
Basic Needs Emergency Grant Non-Qualified Scholarships
The CARES office is open Monday - Thursday 8:00 a.m.- 5:00 p.m. and Friday 9:30 a.m. - 5:00 p.m. Case Managers meet by appointment. View the Make an Appointment Instructions .
Appointments check in at the CARES office, Room 1000 on the 1st floor of The WELL.

Emergency Grants are non-qualified scholarships for students experiencing a one-time unexpected financial hardship meeting their basic needs that is negatively affecting their academics. Please note, emergency grants are counted as part of your total financial aid package, and all financial aid rules apply. To be considered for an emergency grant and learn if your situation meets qualifications, please make an appointment with a CARES case manager by logging into your Student Health Patient Portal and selecting the CARES - Emergency Grant type appointment option, or you can call CARES to request assistance with scheduling at (916) 278-5138.
To submit additional documentation, please use the Secure Document Submission Tool

The Seth Nelsen Student Emergency Grant (x12750) supports students who are experiencing one-time financial challenges. This fund helps cover unexpected expenses that impact a student’s enrollment. This fund is in memory of Seth Nelsen, the son of Sacramento State President Robert Nelsen and his wife Jody Nelsen, who lost their son to suicide. They remain committed to helping students in crisis, such as supporting this fund. Donate to The Student Emergency Grant

The Student Emergency Housing Fund (x12870) supports students who are currently residing in our short-term emergency housing program. Donations to this fund help pay for meals and housing support for students in one-time, unexpected housing emergencies that impact their enrollment. Donate to The Student Emergency Housing Fund
CARES/Basic Needs The WELL, Office 1000 M–Th: 7:30a.m.–4p.m., F: 9a.m.–4p.m.

links.text: Basic Needs Resource Center (BNRC) Open Sep. 2!

pages.title: CARES Emergency Housing Support and Resources | Sacramento State
pages.description: This page provides information about student emergency funds.
headings: Crisis Assistance & Resource Education Support (CARES) Division of Student Affairs
CARES Emergency Housing Support and Resources
Support For Students Affected By Fires
contentBlocks: This service offers up to 30 days of housing in the residence halls located at North Village . This program is for students experiencing a brief, unexpected housing crisis, one-time usage during their academic career.
The Emergency Housing Program is a first come, first serve program based on the current occupancy of four beds.
Application will be prioritized based on need and the student’s circumstances

Waitlist Information: Due to high demand, if no beds are available, the student will be screened by a case manager and added to a waitlist. The student being placed on the waitlist does not guarantee that they will be enrolled in the Emergency Housing Program.
Due to high demand, if no beds are available, the student will be screened by a case manager and added to a waitlist.
The student being placed on the waitlist does not guarantee that they will be enrolled in the Emergency Housing Program.
Must be a currently enrolled student at Sacramento State
Minimum enrollment of six units; priority will be given to full-time students (12+ units)
Students with a conduct history will be reviewed on a case-by-case basis
Students are required to meet with the case manager 1x/week during their stay
No additional family members (i.e., romantic partner, spouse, children, grandparents, etc.)
No pets - service animals and ESAs are allowed with appropriate documentation
Must be able to cohabitate with a roommate
The student may be asked to complete additional screening processes for housing and roommate matching based on the discretion of the case manager.
The student must follow all University Housing Services policies and procedures while residing in the residence halls at North Village
Please initiate your referral by completing the Emergency Housing Interest Form, and a case manager will contact you via your student email.
Complete the Emergency Rehousing Interest Form Please note: The student must complete this form before meeting with a case manager
Please note: The student must complete this form before meeting with a case manager
The student must complete a screening meeting with a case manager Use the "Make An Appointment" button to schedule an appointment and select the EMERGENCY HOUSING appointment type.
Use the "Make An Appointment" button to schedule an appointment and select the EMERGENCY HOUSING appointment type.

Sacramento State's Rapid Rehousing Program utilizes a transitional housing model reserved for students experiencing homelessness and/or significant housing insecurity. The program is designed to offer master leased housing for 3-6 months while students are actively meeting and working with the CARES and contracted Community-Based Organization (CBO) case managers to stabilize their housing, academics, and holistic wellness.

Students who enroll in rapid rehousing agree to participate fully in the program requirements including, but not limited to, paying $500.00/month rent, attending weekly scheduled meetings with CARES and/or CBO case managers, and adhering to house rules and roommate agreements in the rapid rehousing shared living environment. House rules and roommate agreements are arranged by the CBO providing the master leased apartments.

Students who enroll in the rapid rehousing program will be offered a 3-month lease. After the initial 3-month lease, students may be offered a month-to-month lease extension for up to an additional 3 months. Lease extensions after the initial 3-months will include a review of the student’s participation in all program requirements. Lease extensions will not be offered to students who are not actively participating in all required program activities.
Bed Availability in the Rapid Rehousing Program is limited. This program is a first come, first serve program based on the current occupancy and available beds
When applications open, they will be prioritized based on need and the student’s circumstances
Enrolled in a minimum of 12 units (undergraduate student) and 6 units (graduate student) at Sacramento State
Currently experiencing homelessness (street homelessness, sleeping in a car, couch-surfing, staying somewhere not intended for habitation, etc.) OR immediate risk of homelessness
No additional family members (i.e., spouse, children, grandparents, etc.)
Must be employed and able to pay monthly rent
Must be able to cohabitate with two or more roommates
Students are required to meet with the case manager to participate in bi-weekly case management meetings focused on finding stable/permanent housing
Please initiate your referral by completing the Rapid Housing Interest Form, and a case manager will contact you via your student email.
Complete the Rapid Rehousing Interest Form Please note: The student must complete this form before meeting with a case manager
The student must complete a screening meeting with a case manager Use the "Make An Appointment" button to schedule an appointment and select the EMERGENCY & RAPID REHOUSING appointment type.
Use the "Make An Appointment" button to schedule an appointment and select the EMERGENCY & RAPID REHOUSING appointment type.
This program is designed to help students who are at risk of facing an eviction up to a maximum amount of $1500.00

Please note: If a student's Emergency Grant application is approved, these monies may not disburse until 2-3 weeks after their application has been approved. Emergency grants are non-qualified scholarships and will impact a student's total financial aid and Cost of Attendance budget.

Emergency grants are non-qualified scholarships and will impact a student's total financial aid and Cost of Attendance budget.
Currently enrolled in a degree-seeking program at Sacramento State

Other eviction notices from the landlord
Students may be asked to provide one or more of the following documents: Copy of Rent Agreement

This program is designed to help students who have a history of late rent payments, paying partial rental, or are at risk of facing an eviction due to an unanticipated financial hardship
Eligible students receive three months of partial rental assistance. The payments will be issued and mailed directly to your property manager
Please Note: The enrollment process for this program is extensive and may take 3 to 4 weeks to complete The student’s property manager must agree to participate in the Rent Subsidy Program
The enrollment process for this program is extensive and may take 3 to 4 weeks to complete
The student’s property manager must agree to participate in the Rent Subsidy Program
This program is not available during Winter Break and Summer Session

The student must have a history of partial or late rent payments as evidenced by the following: Having been given a three-day notice to pay rent or quit Facing eviction and can provide legal documentation about Court filings related to eviction
Having been given a three-day notice to pay rent or quit
Facing eviction and can provide legal documentation about Court filings related to eviction
The student must be experiencing an unanticipated financial hardship and be able to provide supporting documentation
Enrolled Full-time (undergraduate 12 units; graduate six units)
Students can only be enrolled in this program one time during their enrollment at CSUS.
Must successfully complete an advising session with the Financial Wellness Center
Students will be asked to provide one or more of the following documents:
Other eviction notices from the property manager
Documentation of unexpected financial hardship, such as paystubs reflecting reduced work hours, termination letter from an employer, paperwork reflecting a reduction in salary, etc.
Copy of Rental/Lease Agreement Must include information on the student’s roommates Must include information on the student’s rent portion

W9 form completed by the landlord or property manager This form will be provided to the student after they have been successfully enrolled in the Rapid Rehousing program The student’s landlord or property manager must agree to complete this document for payments to be disbursed and mailed

Visit the Community Housing Resources on the Basic Needs Page

CARES has resources to assist students who are experiencing financial hardship related to the SoCal Fires. Please log into your student health portal and make a CARES Emergency Grant type appointment to meet with a case manager. If you need help scheduling, please call us at (916)278-5138.

The College Initiative is a partnership between Lutheran Social Services (LSS), CSUS and Sacramento County Office of Education (SCOE) serving student parents 18-24 years with dependent children, and who are housing insecure. Services include housing navigation support, rent subsidy, emergency grants, and educational and developmental support for the student parents' children. Please initiate your referral by completing the Student Parent Housing Interest Form and the Housing Navigator will contact you.
Complete the Student Parent Interest Form
Student Parent College Initiative Program

pages.title: CalFresh Outreach Application Assistance | Sacramento State
pages.description: This page provides all the information for the CalFresh nutrition assistance program for low-income students.
headings: Crisis Assistance & Resource Education Support (CARES) Division of Student Affairs
Worried about Immigration or Public Charge Issues?
Student programs exempt from the CalFresh work requirement:
contentBlocks: You may have financial aid to help pay for your education, but do you have food aid? CalFresh helps eligible students buy groceries! CalFresh is a nutrition assistance program that helps low-income individuals and families buy the food they need. CalFresh dollars help stretch a tight food budget and help many people afford to incorporate healthy foods like fruits and vegetables into their diets. CalFresh is the name of the USDA-funded Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) in the state of California. Recipients receive and “spend” CalFresh benefits electronically, via personal Golden State Advantage electronic benefits transfer (EBT) cards that look just like any other credit or debit cards.

CFO is CalFresh Outreach. Sacramento State is a sub-contractor along with other CSU Campuses and community-based organizations to provide CalFresh Outreach and application assistance services. USDA-CDSS funding covers less than one-half of the CalFresh Outreach contract. The remaining funds are provided by non-federal funding sources.
We can help you with the application, uploading your documents, advocacy support between the student and the county office, and answer questions along the way.
We do not have access to the outcome of your application, all questions regarding your final eligibility and amount of benefits can be answered by your county office.
Schedule an appointment for one-on-one support either in-person, or online
The SNAP Retailer Locator allows anyone to locate nearby CalFresh (SNAP)-authorized retailers by entering a street address, city and state or zip code.
Learn about Immigration or Public Charge Issues

The application process takes about 40 minutes and you will be assisted by a trained CalFresh Outreach Intern. Please come prepared with your required documents in electronic form, and if you cannot get all documents at once, don’t worry! You have 30 days to turn in the remaining documents. You can also apply on your own at the CalFresh Website.
Who do you purchase and prepare food with? Use this number of people in your household to determine which row to use on the chart below.
Using this row, is your household under the gross monthly income limit? If you meet these requirements, go on to Question 2.

Are you a college student working in a paid position for at least 20 hours per week OR a total of 80 hours a month on average? If YES , this may meet the work requirement If NO , please see the work requirement exemptions in section 3.
If YES , this may meet the work requirement
If NO , please see the work requirement exemptions in section 3.
Are you younger than 22 years of age AND living your parents or guardians? If YES , stop here and apply for CalFresh benefits with your family. If NO , go to Question 3.
If YES , stop here and apply for CalFresh benefits with your family.
If you are not working in a paid position for at least 20 hours per week OR a total of 80 hours a month on average, you must meet ONE of the following exemptions:

Approved (or awarded or accepted) for federal or state work study, anticipates working, and have not refused a work assignment (can still be eligible even if a work-study job has not begun or is not currently available).

Students attending CSU or UC, receiving Cal Grant A or B, and falling within the following qualifications: unmarried; AND 25 years of age or younger; AND household income less than $50,000 (per FAFSA application).
Enrolled in a state-funded program that increases employability (Project Rebound).

Exerting parental control over a dependent household member under the age of 6 OR between the age of 6 and 12 with no adequate childcare (as determined by the county on a case-by-case basis) OR are a single parent of a dependent household member under the age of 12.
Participating in the CalFresh Employment and Training Program (CFET) or Job Opportunities and Basic Skills (JOBS), or be a recipient of CalWORKs or Aid to Families with Dependent Children (AFDC).

If you are in a program that increases employability and is not listed above, see the full policy list for other approved paid work rule exemptions (e.g. CHC Internship, Stanford Teacher Education Program, etc.) and scroll down to "CalFresh Student Eligibility: Approved Programs to Increase Employability".

International, DACA and DREAM students have been shown to NOT be eligible, even with a work VISA/SSN.
Out-of-state students can still be eligible for CalFresh if they are residing in California for the school year.

Student eligibility is based on enrollment at a higher education institution, which are defined as either: business, trade, technical or vocational schools normally requiring a high school diploma or GED to enroll; or a junior, community, two-year or four-year college or university, or graduate school, regardless whether a high school diploma or GED is required.

Veterans and foster youth may have many different forms of income. The applicant's eligibility worker will help them determine what income counts and what doesn't. All will need to be reported on the application.
For immediate resources please visit our CSUS Basic Needs Website . To set up an application assistance appointment email Sac State CalFresh or call/text 916-400-9027.
You will need to produce a state ID, birth certificate and Social Security Card during the application process. If you need assistance accessing these documents please see the following resources
Driver License Renewals, Duplicates, and Changes
Obtaining Certified Copies of ​​Birth & Death Records
Funded by USDA SNAP, known in California as CalFresh, an equal opportunity provider and employer, and the California Department of Social Services.

pages.title: Emergency Grant Non-Qualified Scholarship Category Criteria | Sacramento State
pages.description: This page provides information about the Emergency Grant Non-Qualified Scholarship Category Criteria.
headings: Crisis Assistance & Resource Education Support (CARES) Division of Student Affairs
Emergency Grant Non-Qualified Scholarship Category Criteria
Utilities (includes Water, Gas, Electric and Internet/Wi-Fi): Up to $300
contentBlocks: You are required to electronically submit at least one documentation for each category of support that you are requesting. Please note, if you are seeking an Eviction Avoidance grant in the housing category, you must provide at least 2 pieces of documentation; your fully executed lease agreement and at least one additional documentation that reflects your rent is past due.

The information below will provide you with examples of the kind of supporting documentation you can provide, as well as the total dollar amount that you may request for each category. You must first make an appointment with a CARES Case Manager who will approve you to apply for specific categories of support based on your assessed needs. The documentation you submit will be used to determine the amount of support you may receive in each category. The maximum support that a student may receive is up to $2100.00 and these grants are available only one time in the academic year for unexpected financial hardships. These grants cannot be used to cover tuition, mandatory fees or university debt.
A letter from your childcare provider verifying the hours of care provided each week, and the total amount due
3 copies of your most recent paystubs that show your work hours were reduced.
A letter from your former employer showing that you were terminated/laid off, or that your work hours were reduced.
Correspondence from a county government office that shows you had a change in benefits under CalFresh or Cash Aid.
For Eviction Avoidance: you must provide multiple documents, as below:
A copy of your signed lease agreement, including the breakdown of each individual resident’s total rental payment due monthly.
A signed letter from your property manager/landlord, or a 3 day pay or quit notice from your property manager/landlord, that shows your total amount due to avoid eviction. For Move-In Support
A copy of your signed lease agreement with a confirmed move in date, including the breakdown of each individual resident’s total rental payment due monthly.
If seeking support to cover security deposits, ensure that the lease agreement includes this information.

Please note: Technology requests can be made when your computer/laptop has been stolen, damaged, or is in need of repair or replacement. Grants cannot be used to replace/repair Air pods/Earphone/Headphone devices gaming devices, and any other devices that are deemed unnecessary to aid in completion of courses.
If your equipment was stolen, provide a copy of a law enforcement report verifying the items were stolen.
Photo images of damaged equipment to be replaced.
If the damaged equipment can be repaired, please provide an estimate of the cost to replace parts and or complete the repair.

Please note: Car insurance is required in the state of California. If you do not have proof of motor vehicle insurance, we cannot assist you with costs to repair your vehicle after an accident. The grant is not allowable to pay car insurance premiums.
Emergency Travel for bereavement or medical emergency

If the need is related to bereavement after the death of a family member or related to a medical emergency, the student will be asked to produce a written statement from a healthcare professional that indicates there is a need for the student to travel.

A document/screenshot of the cost of travel. Travel can be by plane, train, or bus. If you are seeking assistance with covering the costs of fuel, a Google Map search can be submitted to show the mileage from your primary residence to the location where you are going to travel.
Car Safety Repair, Including Damaged or Stolen Parts

Documentation of ownership of the vehicle. If you are using a family/friend’s vehicle, then the owner of the vehicle must write a letter confirming that you have permission to use their vehicle as your primary commuter vehicle.

If you are seeking assistance with a safety vehicle repair, or assistance with costs after a motor vehicle accident, you will need to submit an estimate of the costs for repair and proof of vehicle insurance. The estimate must come from a professional auto-repair business, or your insurance company.
Documentation of any rental car and costs.
If you are seeking assistance with costs of fuel, your primary address of residence must be 50 miles or greater one-way travel to campus, and a Google Map search can be submitted as proof.
A copy of a past due notice for utility costs that indicated that utility service will be suspended to the student’s primary residence, due to lack of payment.
If you have a roommate, then in the narrative of your application please provide a written description of how much you pay to cover the costs of the home utilities.

pages.title: Behavioral Intervention Assistance | Sacramento State
pages.description: The Behavioral Intervention Assistance page provides resources for faculty and staff who may be confronted with situations in which a student is displaying concerning behavior.
headings: Crisis Assistance & Resource Education Support (CARES) Division of Student Affairs
contentBlocks: While interacting with students across the college, faculty and staff may be confronted with situations in which a student is displaying concerning behavior. The Behavioral Intervention Team is a resource available to the campus to address students of concern. The Behavioral Intervention Team is a multidisciplinary team, which meets regularly to monitor reports of students of concern and track patterns, behaviors, and disturbances to groups or individuals. The team can perform a threat assessment and determines the appropriate response and mechanisms to intervene and respond to a crisis or concerning behaviors.
Notify Student Affairs of a student in distress or distressing student using the button below.
The Sacramento State Crisis Intervention team consists of representatives from the following areas of campus:
Associate Vice President of Student Affairs
Student Affairs Director of Student Conduct
Behaviors of concern are actions that have the potential to compromise the safety of the campus community. Some examples are:
Threats of harm to others (including electronically, in a paper or essay, or verbalized to another person)
Talk of suicide or suicidal action (including electronically, in a paper or essay, or verbalized to another person)
Erratic behavior (including online activities) that disrupts the mission and/or normal proceedings of University students, faculty, staff, or community.
Significant inappropriate disruption to the community
Odd or concerning behaviors that you have a "gut feeling" about with regards to student or campus safety

Behaviors of concern might be observed in a number of settings: in the classroom, at a service location or office, at an on-campus job, or during participation at an extracurricular activity. By reporting behaviors that are concerning, the Behavioral Intervention Team will be able to reach out to students to intervene, provide support, and connect them with resources that can assist them.
